org: Royal Borough of Kingston upon Thames

candidates.stmt:
Ian Manders has lived in Raeburn Avenue since 2013.

When previously a local councillor, Ian introduced dog waste bins into the borough’s parks, making Kingston Council one of the first in UK. Ian was Chair of New Malden Neighbourhood committee for four years, with responsibility for housing, street services and the Malden Centre.

In his professionallife, Ian is an expert on green energy, and organised successful campaigns for an Act of Parliament which empowered councils to demand property developers build to higher energy standards and install renewable energy.

With the exception of his student days at Manchester University studying Chemical Engineering, Simon has lived in Kingston Borough all his adult life. Simon's time at Chessington has been spent at two CCCs. Fifteen enjoyable years bowling for the mighty Chessington Cricket club and his first job was as a software engineer at Chessington Computer Centre. Since then Simon has continued to work in computing, managing software development for global applications. Simon's children are now reaching the final stages of their education and he feels it is the right time to try and give something back to a borough that has given so much to him and his family.

**A Vote for Aaron is a Vote for You**

Year after year, the same parties have let us down. What was once a strong community now feels overlooked, underfunded, and ignored and I'm tired of it.

I'm tired of watching our hard-earned council tax disappear into dodgy decisions made by people who've never had to worry about keeping a roof over their heads, juggling work and family, or sitting in a waiting room for hours.

I have a daughter and a partner. All we want is what previous generations had decent pay, a home, clean water, affordable bills, and services that actually work. That shouldn't be too much to ask.

As your local councillor, I won't be answering to a party. No whips telling me how to vote. No ladders to climb. No boxes to tick for Westminster. Just you the people of Alexandra ward telling me what matters, and me fighting for it.

And I mean that literally. I will be the first councillor in this area to introduce a simple, open system that gives every resident a real say in local decisions. Not just a vote every four years an actual voice, all year round.

This isn't about politics as usual. This is about taking our community back.

**If you work hard and play by the rules, you deserve a councillor who does the same.**

Vote Aaron  Independent, Accountable, and Only Answerable to You.

I have lived in Tolworth (and Alexandra ward) for 3 years and I’m keen to see a Labour voice in the Council. With a young family myself, my priorities are to ensure that services are maintained for children, families and young people and that our community assets are protected and enhanced for the benefit of all.

Steve has lived in Kingston since 2013, having originally moved to the borough to study at Kingston University. Steve's past employment includes working for the Princess Alice Hospice in Kingston and he now works in student services at Kingston University. He is currently finishing corrections to his PhD thesis in International Relations concerning historic Anglo-Japanese relations.

Since 2022, Steve has been a vocal campaigner on the reconstruction of the Kingfisher Leisure Centre in Kingston. In addition, he has campaigned on issues such as retaining Kingston’s tree planting scheme and increasing recycling within the Borough. Steve is keen on addressing Kingston’s affordable housing shortage, improving green spaces, community resilience and preserving local historical assets.

Paul has lived in Beverley Ward for fifteen years. His daughter attends Burlington School. In order to improve the local area, he successfully campaigned for new dog bins in Beverley. He continues to campaign for investment in the High Street, safety in our local parks and better roads and pavments. As a Neighbourhood Watch Cooridnator, Paul appreciates the importance of bringing a community together to reduce crime.

Background in education. 
Work with Kingston Race Equality Council.
Very interested in how we support all the creative arts in schools and wider society.

I have lived in Surbiton for 40 years after coming to study for my degree in Applied Chemistry at the then Kingston Polytechnic. I work as a Product Manager for a chemical company now mainly from home in Surbiton. I enjoy walking and swimming and have completed several long distance walks including the South Downs Way. I believe passionately in a fair society with rights and decency for all people and believe Labour can revitalise Kingston.

The reason why I am standing as a councillor is that I’ve always strongly believed in the sense of community and change from the ground up… not top down. These are more than words and are demonstrated by my actions of the past few years. 
 1.  I have been serving on the committee of the Canbury and Riverside Association (CARA) and currently chair those meetings.
 2.  I’m a regular contributor to CARA newsletters and occasional editor of the online newsletter.
 3.  I sit on the Canbury Gardens Working Group which works with council on all types of issues across the entire ward.
 4.  I am also a committee member of Friends of Canbury Gardens.
 5.  I also serve on the Friends of Canbury Bandstand working group. 
6.  As well as working with existing councillors, I have also been working with the police and have been a member of the North Kingston Police Panel for the past three years.
7.  I attend Voice of the River meetings and,
8.  An active contributor to the ‘Drinkable Thames’ project.

We are very fortunate to live in this region.  I am doing this to help the community of Canbury Gardens primarily, but also represent all constituents across the entire Borough of Kingston.

I'm proud to be standing in 2026 on the Kingston Labour Manifesto, **A borough that works for everyne".** This includes the following ...

"The Labour government and the Mayor of London have been working hard to repair over a decade of cuts and neglect. That work includes major investment in public services and infrastructure, from estate safety funding and investment in Kingston Hospital, to more money for roads and highways, and expanding the Warm Homes Discount to support more households with the cost of energy.

"But too often Kingston misses out on key funding opportunities, and local residents are not seeing the full benefit of the support available from Labour in government and City Hall. Kingston deserves a stronger Labour voice locally, one that works in partnership with national and regional government to deliver the investment and change our borough needs."

Dear neighbour,
My name is Ali, I’m standing in the upcoming Council elections as an independent because our community needs someone who gets things done.
My background is in project delivery, working on infrastructure and construction projects where results matter. I’ve managed budgets, coordinated teams, solved problems on the ground, and seen projects through from start to finish. That means I’m used to dealing with real issues, not passing them on.
That’s exactly how I’ll approach this role.
When something needs fixing, whether it’s a local safety concern, ongoing maintenance issue, or a problem affecting residents’ day to day, I won’t wait around. I’ll be visible, involved, and focused on practical solutions that make a difference.
I care about protecting the quality of life in our area: safe streets, well-maintained public spaces, and a community people are proud to live in. But that only happens when someone is willing to take responsibility and follow things through properly.
I will:
• Be present in the community and easy to reach
• Act quickly on local issues and see them through
• Work directly with residents, officers, and services to fix problems
• Focus on outcomes, not excuses
This isn’t about politics for me, it’s about putting in the work, being accountable, and delivering real improvements.
If you want a councillor who is hands-on, responsive, and committed to getting things done, I’m ready to step up. Please lend ONE of your two votes to me, a true local Independent resident, whose first and only priority is you - my neighbours.
I will see you around, lets have a chat.
Ali

Sue has lived and grown her business in Chessington South over the last 28 years and for the last 18 years has been a member of the Federation of Small Businesses (FSB) - last 8 of them as a branch chairman and activist. Sue has lobbied MP's in the FSB branch area - Chris Grayling, Paul Scully, Dominic Raab, Sir Paul Beresford, James Berry and Ed Davey! Sue has also made contact with the Councils - Kingston, Elmbridge, Mole Valley, Leatherhead and Sutton. The changes in the FSB made sue decide to cross the divide and help in a different way. She wants to be more involved locally and try to make Chessington South a major part of the Conservative idea of a Better, Brighter Borough.

candidates.p: The Official Monster Raving Loony Party
The Official Monster Raving Loony Party
candidates.stmt:
The ONLY wasted vote is one that isn't used!

Forget the Spinners - Vote for Chinners

Rowena moved to Kingston over 30 years ago, making a home with her husband and three daughters, who attended Coombe Hill Infant and Junior Schools and Coombe Girls.

Rowena knows the ward well, having initially lived with her family in Manorgate Road and now Dickerage Road.

She qualified as a Chartered Accountant with KPMG in London and has extensive financial experience both in the private and public sector.

Keen to give back to the community Rowena helped run a local Girl Guiding unit and served as a governor of Robin Hood Primary for 8 years. She is now a Trustee of Coombe Multi Academy Trust and also a Director of Kingston Chamber of Commerce.

Rowena said: “It has been a huge honour to serve Coombe Hill ward as a councillor and I would very much like to carry on helping residents across this wonderful ward and holding the LibDem administration to account.”

Ian moved to Kingston 35 years ago, for a new job in central Kingston. He has lived locally in Coombe Hill for many years. Firstly on Kingston Hill, and more recently just off Kingston Hill in Crescent Road where he has been instrumental in opposing the road closure. Ian voted against the road closure from the start, and although he is entitled to a resident pass to drive through the barrier, he has always refused one on principle.

Ian likes to support local groups, and is a collector for the annual Poppy Appeal. He also supports Kingston Churches Action on Homelessness (KCAH) with their fundraising - including taking part in several sponsored sleep-outs.
 
Ian said:  “I am lucky to live locally, and have got to know many local residents. I would love to continue, and with your support we can deal with those important local issues, such as potholes, roadworks, crime and keeping our streets clean, that LibDem-run Kingston Council is failing on.”

I have always thought that, locally as well as nationally, the Labour Party offers the best chance of building a fair and prosperous society.  My first job was with the research team at Labour Party headquarters. I have been involved in campaigning and policy work ever since, while working on in international development studies and analysis of markets.  I lived in Coombe since the 1980s, and want the Council to make sure that developments preserve and enhance amenities enjoyed by the entire population.  With Tories in central government still to make up for their attacks since 2010 on local authority finances, councillors have to concentrate on cutting waste, giving the highest priority to meeting social needs and mitigating the effects of lockdown on the wider educational needs of children in the borough.

I am a longstanding Kingston resident and was educated at Coombe Girls School and London University.  I am dismayed by the trashing of Kingston’s built environment by developers who have no interest in making Kingston a better place - only in making money.  Luxury flats do nothing to alleviate the shortage of decent housing for Kingston people.  I promise to oppose unsympathetic development and to campaign for quality affordable housing with transport links and services.  Labour stands for better local services, investment in children and young people, support for local businesses, whether service, retail or manufacturing, and a creative quarter that would make the most of our unique history and create jobs. Labour can win in Kingston.  All it takes is your votes.  Kingston deserves more than just more cuts to services and more tower blocks.  Please vote Labour – the real alternative.

Phil has lived in the borough with his wife Marie for almost 20 years. Two of his three children live nearby with their own children. Following his retirement as a police superintendent, he established a licensing and planning business and is now one of the country's leading licensing experts. He has lectured in licensing law at Westminster University and has given evidence to Parliamentary Committees. Phil takes a keen interest in local issues and attends St Andrews Church. In particular he is concerned about cutting crime and disorder, making the streets safe and clean and reducing the highest council tax in London.

Coombe Vale has been home to my partner and me for 25 years, and our son attends a local state secondary school.
For most of this time, I have worked in and around Parliament, with the past 17 years spent advising senior members of the Labour Party. Before that, I headed up communications for a local government campaign group and worked in public affairs. Kingston needs Labour councillors to deliver our Labour Government’s plan for change.

After 24 successful years as a Merton Councillor, I moved to Kingston to live with my partner. As a Councillor, I served in many roles, including Cabinet Member for Sport & Leisure. I was responsible for rebuilding Colliers Wood Library at no cost to the taxpayer and delivering Morden Leisure Centre, almost identical to the new Kingfisher, but with no loss of service, very little delay, and at a fraction of the cost of what we are seeing in Kingston.

I now lead a quieter life, helping at the CREST Tuesday food bank, presenting popular and often chaotic rhyme times for children twice a week in Mitcham, and looking after my three grandchildren. I am also a keen walker. I followed the Grand Union Canal from London to Birmingham last year, and I am currently walking from Rome to Lecce in Southern Italy in stages.

I enjoy working with people and staying active. I bring a practical, imaginative, and cost-effective approach to everything I do. I believe Kingston would be better served with me as a Labour Councillor.

My family and I have lived in Coombe Vale for over a decade.  We’ve seen Tory councillors here and a Tory controlled council, and now we’ve seen Lib Dem councillors here in Coombe Vale with a Lib Dem controlled council, and nothing has changed.  Our daughter goes to the local state school in Coombe Vale and we know first-hand the way the council has failed to give strong leadership to education in the borough.  It’s time for real change; both nationally and locally it’s only the Labour Party that can renew our country.

I have lived in the borough of Kingston since 1974 and in New Malden for over 30 years. Since my retirement from primary school teaching in 2016 one of the first things I did was to become a Labour party member, becoming active in the party shortly afterwards as a local election candidate and as joint secretary for Kingston and Surbiton CLP. My other community involvement is with Kingston Environment Centre where after becoming a director in 2019 I recently took over as Chairperson. I am also the lead volunteer on our recycling, gardening and weekly Community Fridge projects.
One of my main priorities as Chairperson has been to make good use of the building as a low cost, inclusive meeting place for a wide range of community groups so that now we have an almost full timetable with cycling, mental health, exercise and senior citizens groups meeting regularly as well as the Square1 café and an after school club.
My background with Kingston Environment Centre and other voluntary groups has given me an insight into how many people in New Malden are struggling financially, with mental health issues, poor living conditions, council services that do not work for them, traffic congestion, leading to increased air pollution and a distinct lack of provision for children and young people that does not cost a fortune. I believe that Labour has the best policies to tackle these issues.
Taking other Labour councils in South West London as a lead as a Labour councillor I would push for genuine value for money for residents. Thousands of pounds have been wasted on plans and consultations without any results, the Fountain Roundabout being a prime example of this. I would also work on communicating council decisions effectively to residents. As the local Labour manifesto says:
Labour will aim to “Improve transparency and democracy by giving residents clear, accessible information on council structures and how to contact officers, so everyone can engage effectively with local decision

Having lived for most of my adult life in New Malden and raised a family here. I have watched with growing concern how our Liberal Democrats Council (they have 42 out 48 seats at the Guildhall) is giving free rein to private developers with no comprehensive plan for New Malden.

As a result today’s children will struggle to find affordable housing in ever more, oversized developments.

Aside from the City of London Corporation, Kingston has the worst record in London for the provision of social housing. The average wait time for a three-bed affordable home in Kingston is now over 25 years! In addition despite the extra Government funding to fix Kingston’s poor roads, our Royal Borough has been assessed with a red rating (the lowest) for road maintenance. And yet we are charged some of the highest rates of Council Tax in London.

Across the By-pass in neighbouring Merton (a Labour-run borough) residents pay £400 less a year for their council services.  Our Council can and should do more.

Vote Labour on May 7th and we will show you how.

The election on 7th May provides a crucial opportunity to vote for a desperately needed improvement in governance and financial management. 
The litany of failure and ineptitude inflicted upon the borough by the Liberal Democrats has had a demonstrably detrimental effect on the area. 
Let’s vote to end a protracted and ineffectual period of quasi-one party local government.

The highest Council Tax in London, and a conspicuous dearth of justifications, let alone improvements.

This must stop!

I’m Stephen Cherry. A Kingston resident of 30 years, with decades of experience in international finance, and leadership positions, from which I have gained considerable knowledge in astute financial management and the most effective methods in delivering positive results. 
I stand ready to steer Kingston Council toward a better future.

David moved to Hook and Chessington North in 2013, drawn by the promise of green spaces and good links into London. After 18 years as a civil servant — most recently championing the safe and equitable development of self-driving technologies — he is stepping into a new chapter, and standing for the Green Party feels like the natural next step.
'Chessington is one of the poorer wards in the borough, and too often it feels like decisions are made elsewhere, for elsewhere. As a Green Party councillor, I won't be beholden to the Lib Dem majority — I can speak up for our community and put Chessington's needs first.'
'Our ward is crying out for better public transport — too many journeys that should be a quick bus ride are either hopelessly convoluted or just easier by car. And when it comes to planning, major developments keep being waved through with little thought for the impact on our green spaces, our local services, or the people who already live here. That has to change.'

I’ve spent my working life in education and international development, and I have lived in Kingston for 15 years, having moved from the borough of Lewisham to be near family.

My roots are in a Welsh mining village and Liverpool, so standing up to power is in my DNA. I have been a Labour supporter all my life, though I was happily married for many years to a Liberal Democrat supporter.

I believe we urgently need a return to a more polite and rational political discourse, and that is even more important now than it was three years ago. We need calm thinking, careful analysis and practical solutions rather than slogans and rhetoric, and a commitment to helping those who are less well off.

Amongst the many challenges we face, the overriding one is the Climate Emergency, even while the cost of living is front of mind for many families. Better-insulated homes would make a real difference, reducing both energy bills and emissions.

I spent much of my adult life living and working in Africa, and have worked as a teacher and in international development.

I am active in faith, community and charity affairs.

In my spare time I play bridge and visit Kew Gardens.

candidates.p: The Official Monster Raving Loony Party

candidates.stmt:
I am a lifelong local resident and have dedicated over a decade to serving my community, playing an active role in supporting and enhancing local spaces and schools, through involvement in a park Friends group and school PTAs. I have held  Treasurer and Chair positions throughout this period.

I am passionate about protecting the environment and preserving the character of our local neighbourhoods, working to bring people together and strengthen community relationships. I have regularly organised large-scale free community events in Old Malden, campaigned on highway issues and supported on setting up school streets.

Alongside my community work, I am a finance professional and a mother of two, balancing my career, family life, and civic responsibility with energy and care.

My decision to stand for election is driven by frustration with the inaction of current ward councillors and a firm belief that our community deserves more proactive, responsive representation.

Gia Borg Darcy - Putting You First

I am a mum, business owner, and community leader. 15 years in Kingston, wife to Michael, mum to three beautiful daughters. Founder of a private Tuition business and active in our community as Chairman of Kingston & Surbiton Conservatives as well as police ward panel Chairman, former school Governor and a volunteer for local community lead events. Loves singing choral music locally, Fighting for transparency, SEND, our environment and supporting local businesses, but most of all holding the current council to account. Prioritising our community's needs, not party politics.

I have lived with my family in Kingston for 15 years. My children go to school locally and I am a trustee at The Kingston Academy, an amazing school.

I am very proud of Kingston, I think it is unique. Our schools, university, hospital, the town centre, the river, the green spaces, the people. We are a city in a city.

The next decade will shape us. The world is changing, Kingston is changing, we can all see this. We need to grip the things that the council can control: housing, new developments, education, services for the vulnerable.

What will improve things? Strong leadership in the council, people not afraid to ask the hard questions, to challenge the status quo, and with the ability to find solutions that work. Why is our council tax so high when we can’t even manage to have a swimming pool for our children?

Let me tell you about my politics. It’s basically about making and doing. Not making do.

What’s true for Kingston is true for the country.

Kingston has a proud heritage of aviation manufacturing. We used to make things here, products that were the envy of the world. We can still do this, but there is now a real urgency to get making. I believe the council should focus on balancing residential development with encouraging employers to relocate here, who wouldn’t want to walk to work in Kingston?

We need to do, with ambition. There are too many voices demeaning London, Kingston and the country. Yes, we have problems, but we solve them by doing things, not talking about it. Vote me on to the council and I promise I will focus the whole council on doing.

The Labour party is not perfect, but it is laser focused on delivering a better life for everyone, not the few, not just a chosen few. That’s why I am Labour.

Socialism for me is about collective and individual responsibility. Wealth and power are generated from society, not just individual efforts, that’s why we must govern for all. It is not about handouts, it is about support when you need it, and con

I live and work in the Kingston Gate ward, running a business which ensures Artificial Intelligence in healthcare applications is used safely and responsibly. I love this neighbourhood and contribute to it in many ways, for example as an administrator in the North Kingston WhatsApp Support Group and as a participant in other local groups. We live in a beautiful and culturally rich part of London, and are fortunate to have benefitted from Mayor Khan's leadership, our diverse communities and our enterprising residents to ensure London remains the best major city in the world.

Kingston has been my home since I moved here to study at Kingston University in 2008, and it’s where I’ve built my life, my work in the community, and where I am now raising my lovely daughter. I love Kingston’s strong sense of community, its diverse culture and rich heritage, and the beautiful green spaces and riverside we are lucky to enjoy.

Kingston residents have now experienced two full terms of Liberal Democrat council control. Labour believes Kingston should be a borough that puts community at the heart of decision-making — a place where everyone who lives and works here can thrive.

I am proud to stand on the platform of our local manifesto: “A Borough That Works for Everyone.” Real change happens when national progress and local action work together.

I worked in the voluntary sector in Kingston for over a decade and currently serve as a trustee of a local disability charity. I have also worked with the Kingston and Richmond NHS UNISON branch, supporting health workers and advocating for strong public services. Through this work I see every day how vital our local services and community organisations are.

I have been an active campaigner for equal rights, and if elected I will champion access and inclusion across our borough. Disabled people should never be excluded from participating fully in community life, yet too often they still are. I want to help change that so Kingston truly works for everyone.

I am passionate about tackling issues that matter deeply to residents. I have campaigned on environmental issues such as sewage pollution in our rivers, including visiting the Hogsmill sewage treatment works, because our natural spaces must be protected for future generations. I have also campaigned nationally to improve access to affordable childcare so families can thrive and parents are properly supported. I am proud that Labour is delivering support such as free school meals for those who need them and new breakfast clubs for children.

Housing is anot

I don’t think our council of 42 Lib Dems has listened to residents in a long time. I want to see a council that focuses on:

- help with the cost of living
- building communities, not skyscrapers
- clean, green and safe streets

I’m standing to help stop the overdevelopment of central Kingston. Plans for a twenty storey tower block and the overdevelopment of Middle Mill are unacceptable and would make residents lives a misery. These plans do not respect the special character of the area.

- About me

I’ve lived in Kingston upon Thames for 20 years and I’ve loved it from day one! We have an amazing community here.

You might have seen me over the years in the local parks or walking, very slowly(!), down the streets with my basset hound Rosie.

One of the great things about having a dog, particularly a very friendly, very slow walker like a basset, is the chance it gives you to interact with people every day. You see what’s going on in the area, hear some of the local gossip and get to know what’s on people’s minds. It is one of my favourite ways of feeling part of the Kingston community.

I work for the NHS and it is an honour to be part of something I feel is best about Britain and its people. Like many, I owe my life to the NHS. It was there for me when I was diagnosed with cancer and I will never forget the debt I owe to the amazing care and support I received from the doctors and nurses as I fought the hardest battle of my life.

That’s why I use the time I have been given back to fight hard for other people who might need my help.

- Why I am standing

I’m standing for Kingston Town because I want to help make things better for people. I believe strongly that we can achieve great things when we all work together.

Having previously worked with a number of public sector organisations and businesses, I have first hand experience of how to get things done and ensure prompt action from the likes of Kingston Council, Transport for London, etc.

I have led campaigns t

Having lived around Kingston all my life, I've always loved the strong encouragement of arts, our beautiful green spaces, and the diversity of shops and cuisines. Since moving in to the borough itself, I find myself falling in love with the communities and opportunities around, and I endeavour to continue bringing out and developing the best Kingston Town has to offer.

Coming from a low-income background, and being care experienced, I find myself connecting with residents all around Kingston about the struggles faced as a result of poor services, high council tax, and worries about the future if these conditions continue. As a renter, I understand that soaring housing prices only add to those fears. Kingston Town needs more affordable and social housing, and services which are worth their costs. Following the examples of local Labour boroughs like Merton, I aim to push the council towards council-run services, and a local plan with social and affordable housing as a priority. We can see all over Kingston that when profit comes before people, we get unaffordable high rises that drive local residents away instead of housing them. Alongside my colleagues, I aim to put residents and their needs at the forefront, rather than modern looking housing which serves no one.

As the eldest of four, I see firsthand the impact of declining spaces for children and young people. I've been there, trying to find places for my siblings to attend youth clubs or teen-friendly events, and I share the same concerns as so many parents and grandparents; we aren't providing enough for our children, and this must change. As someone who was never a fan of sports, due my disability, I worry for the young people like me, who don't want to or cannot participate in sports clubs, whose only options are to find parks to hang around at with friends. In a borough with such love of street art, theatre, and diverse festivals and musical events, we don't provide nearly enough of these opportunities to o

I have lived in Motspur Park and Old Malden East all my life. I grew up in our local schools and now work as a lecturer at the University of London. This community has shaped who I am, and I am standing as a candidate to be a strong voice for our residents on the council.

We are out knocking on doors, listening to residents, and understanding both the joys and the challenges facing our area. I know how much people care about our neighbourhood, and I share that pride.

I want to bring fresh energy and new ideas to our ward, while respecting and protecting the things we value most. I will raise our concerns with the Mayor of London over the gas holders development, tackle parking problems and graffiti, and work hard to improve our streets and public spaces in a practical and caring way.

If elected, I will offer consistency, availability, hard work, and a genuine commitment to delivering results for our community.

Terry has lived in Motspur Park for over thirty years his children attended local schools. He was a Met police officer, working across London in specialised areas. He now runs a small business locally and continues to use his experience working with local Police, as Safer Neighbourhood Board Vice Chair locally and as Chair For The Royal Borough of Kingston’s Safer Neighbourhood Partnership, working with Police, and other community sectors across the Borough.
Always encouraging residents to join ‘Met Engage’, Terry also volunteers locally and has been working hard on neglected parts of his ward MPOME.
Supporting local residents, the local environment & small business is his passion.
Terry describes his local area as neglected.

I’ve lived in the borough for 14 years, and like many residents I’ve seen both what makes this a great place to live and where things could be done better. As someone with a family here, using local schools and services, I understand the pressures people are facing — from the cost of living to the strain on local services — and the feeling that too often people are paying more but not getting the value they should.

I’m standing as a Labour candidate to give residents a stronger voice locally. I care about this area and the people in it, and I want to see a council that listens properly and focuses on the things that matter day to day — decent housing, clean and safe streets, and reliable local services.

Labour’s plan is about delivering a fairer, greener and more caring borough, with better value for money and real investment in our communities.

If you want a council that works better for residents, I’d be grateful for your support.

I have lived in New Malden for 8 years and as well as being a Captain for a major UK airline I am a senior representative for the pilots’ union and have worked both nationally and internationally to make the aviation industry safer and greener. As a Labour councillor I will represent the views and needs of all Motspur Park and Old Malden residents – not just property developers and private landlords. The current Lib Dem run council charges £400 more per year in tax than the Labour council in neighbouring Merton, and that money should be in your pocket. Your Labour team are on your side, whether that’s in preserving the character of the High Street or making sure any new homes are truly affordable for residents. Only Labour has a plan for Kingston that protects and invests in our public infrastructure, rather than auctioning it off to the highest bidder.

Mark Durrant:

Mark is a councillor for the Liberal Democrats in New Malden Village. He believes in the Lib Dem vision of a fair deal for all.

Mark is a Councillor, MPs Caseworker, and former Media Executive who lives in London, with his wife, a Tutor, and  former Maths Teacher.

He is a passionate believer in local communities working together for the common good. He was honoured to be re-elected to serve his community after running successful campaigns in the UK Local Elections of 2026, in a special election in 2022 and in the UK local elections in 2018.  He believes in housing with good planning and good access to doctors, dentists, and good schooling. He is proud to be part of an administration in Kingston that is building the largest number of new council houses for over 40 years. He serves on the committee that is overseeing  the renewal of the Cambridge Road Housing Estate in Kingston, which includes over 800 much needed affordable and council homes. He also served on the Corporate and Resources Committee which oversees the budgeting for the council,the pension panel, and the Children and Adult Social Care and Education Committee.  Within his own ward he has overseen a significant improvements in the local parks including new playgrounds in all three of our local open spaces, and a significant investment to bring the pavilion at Beverley Park back into use as a much loved cafe. At the local train station Mark has advocated for a newly installed waiting room on platform 4 with another new waiting room coming on platform 1.

He is most proud of helping to get the funding and see the building of a new Burlington Junior School - a £32 million investment in our children’s future

He is a seasoned campaigner having won his own seat three times. He is well known to local residents, and this has come about by being an active member of his local community, regular canvassing, and intelligent use of social media to get across local news.

He works for Paul Kohler MP a

Raju has lived in Surbiton since 1968. He studied at Hollyfield Secondary School and worked for BT until he took early release in March 2012. His community voluntary work over the past thirty-five years includes as Local Authority school governor, and as an elected Councillor in Kingston from 1994 to 2002. He was a founder member of the Kingston Group for Religious Understanding which has now become Kingston Racial Equality Council.

Lucy has lived most of her life in New Malden. A teacher by profession, she has also volunteered locally with Kingston Hospital, the Kingston Environment Centre and the Malden & Coombe Horticultural Society, as well as organising litter picks with the Green Party. Lucy stood for the Green Party in New Malden in the Council elections of 2022. With 10% of the votes, she was in fourth place after the three Lib Dem Councillors who were elected.

"I loved growing up in New Malden and chose to come back here to raise my own family. New Malden is a wonderful place, but when I've spoken to residents, many people are unhappy with the level of overdevelopment here, and feel that New Malden has been taken for granted as somewhere which can just absorb more buildings. If I were elected as Councillor I would stand up for the interests of local people and do my best to keep New Malden as a great place to live. For example, the Sure Start centre in Burlington Road was so helpful for me as a new parent. This has now sadly gone, and if I were elected as Councillor, I'd campaign for New Malden to get one of the new Best Start Family Hubs which are currently being rolled out.

As a teacher, I've had the privilege of working with students who are preparing for their A levels and university applications, and I've seen the immense amount of pressure our young people are under. If I were elected as Councillor I'd campaign for the needs of young people and families, including their mental health, to be prioritised. Of course, as a Green, I understand the vital importance of green spaces for our mental health - protecting our parks and our natural environment is a top priority for me too."

I was born in Fulham and lived in rented accommodation with my Mum, but with the help of a local councillor we got a flat on the Alton Estate in Roehampton. This provided secure housing for my Mum to raise a family. In the early 1980’s I worked at The Victoria and Albert Museum where I was active with the Civil and Public Services Association Union. From 1987-2005 I worked at the Post Office and was Assistant Secretary for our Union Branch. From 2005 I ran the family business, a residential moorings, in Hammersmith for ten years. During this time I volunteered for Beanstalk, a charity that helps children with reading difficulties. I also raised my son as a single parent. From my early personal experience social housing is key to building stronger, secure communities, so we must build more and take care of what we have.

Emily Davey, 47 (as of April 2018), is a housing solicitor with over 20 years' experience working for tenants and social landlords. She has also conducted training for Shelter and the Chartered Institute of Housing.
Emily lives in Kingston with her husband, Ed (MP for Kingston & Surbiton), and their children, John, 10, and Ellie, 4.
Having spent the last two years helping people in Norbiton with legal advice, she is determined to fight for a better housing deal for local residents.
Emily was central to Ed's campaign last June, when the Liberal Democrats once again beat the Conservatives in Kingston & Surbiton.

Olly is 46 (as of April, 2018) and earns his living as a drinks journalist. He has lived in Norbiton for six years with his wife, Jo, and their seven-year-old son, Conor.
Olly also has a dalmatian, Beano, who can be seen being walked twice-daily all around Norbiton.
Olly is a firm believer in offering support when and where support is needed. He also has a passion for environmental issues and a strong interest in education - currently at primary level!

I’m a journalist who has lived and worked in Kingston for most of my life. I’m married and raised my two children in the borough, which means I have a strong personal connection to the community and the issues local families face.

I’m passionate about improving everyday life for residents in Norbiton. I care deeply about creating better opportunities for young people in Kingston and believe that investing in youth services, education, and positive activities is key to building a stronger community.

I’m also a strong supporter of grassroots sport and local facilities, recognising the vital role they play in bringing people together, supporting health and wellbeing, and giving young people positive opportunities to thrive.

I’ve lived in Kingston for 40 years and since I retired I have been active in a residents’ association, in community gardening, and chaired Healthwatch Kingston, the local watchdog for health and social care, where I sat on two council committees and got to know several councillors. In 2024 I was elected as a governor for Kingston and Richmond Foundation Trust (including Kingston Hospital). In 2025 I was given a Mayor’s Community Award- my nominator said ‘she seeks practical compromise but she has a terrier-like determination’, qualities which I hope to put to the service of residents in Norbiton.

I started my career as a public health nurse and then moved into university teaching with a spell at the Department of Health. So I have a great interest in health and social care, and in how we can work together to improve living conditions  and life chances, particularly for babies and young children. It is a disgrace that so many children live in poverty in Kingston, and aren’t  ‘school ready’. At the other end of the spectrum, I am an Age Friendly Ambassador and keen that the move to digital does not exclude older people from participating in local democracy.

Why I Am Standing

As a long-standing Labour Party member I am proud to represent my party in this election and would love to put my skills and experience to use in Norbiton. Having been a senior member of a university managing NHS training contracts I would bring experience of getting things done, including committee work. As a research manager at the Department of Health I worked on evidence-based policy making and I notice how RBK committee papers rarely draw on any research evidence, or comment on how other local authorities tackle the difficult problems that they all face. We can all learn so much from each other.

I love and care about Norbiton. Our community is our strength. But we need to protect our local democracy.

I’ve heard on the doorsteps

-	how services have declined while your council tax keeps rising;

-	how critically important cycle lanes have been designed so badly that they endanger cyclists and pedestrians;

-	that many of you are worried about the decline of your communities as fly-tipping is out of control; and

-	that you don’t feel you have a voice in the key Council decisions that affect you.

We need to change this.

For too long, decisions in Kingston Council have been made behind closed doors.

I believe that when people are trusted and have a direct say over how council tax is spent, our communities and our local democracy will grow stronger.

Labour is committed to giving you the power to decide how money is spent in your neighbourhoods under our manifesto commitment ‘Your Money, Your Choice’.

This direct democracy has been introduced in Labour-run councils in Camden and Newham. It’s been a huge success. It challenges the status quo. It offers an alternative to the traditional top-down relationship between councils and residents. It puts power back in your hands and allows you to work as a community.

At its heart, the 7 May 2026 local election is a choice about values.

1.	The Lib Dems charge you for removing bulky waste, which unfairly punishes people on low incomes and fuels fly-tipping.

LABOUR IS COMMITTED TO INTRODUCING A FREE NEIGHBOURHOOD SKIP SERVICE TO END THIS UNJUST AND PROFOUNDLY UNEQUAL POLICY.

2.	Lib Dem Council decision-making is opaque. Many of you feel you are being ignored and you don’t have a voice in how Council Tax (the most expensive in London) is spent.

LABOUR IS COMMITTED TO DIRECT DEMOCRACY AND GIVING YOU POWER OVER HOW COUNCIL TAX IS SPENT IN YOUR COMMUNITY.

3.	The Lib Dem Council has designed cycle lanes which endanger everyone – pedestrians, cyclists and drivers.

LABOUR WILL PUT CYCLIST AND PEDESTRIAN SAFETY FIRST A

Kevin was born in New Malden and brought up in Old Malden having attended Burlington and Beverley Schools. He is a school Governor in Kingston and has run and established a number of innovative charities in the education sector. He has run a Kingston-based business as well as being the Chairman of a Trust in the centre of Kingston that is raising the profile of Kingston as a place of national historic importance.

Mike Massimi serves as a councillor for Old Malden in the Royal Borough of Kingston upon Thames, where he has earned a strong reputation as a visible, hands-on representative with a clear focus on delivery and real results for local residents.

Over several years he has worked closely with fellow councillor Elizabeth Park, between them raising more than 1,100 resident enquiries and casework issues (by far the highest across the Borough). 
His approach has always prioritised practical action over promises, concentrating on what can genuinely be improved on the ground and then seeing it through.

Community safety has been a particular priority. Through regular ward walks with police, early intervention on environmental and street issues, and sustained engagement with residents, Old Malden has seen measurable and meaningful improvements. 
By the end of 2025, the ward ranked as the safest in London for thefts, burglaries and robberies, placing first out of more than 625 wards across the capital according to Metropolitan Police data, measured both by total crime and by rate per 1,000 residents.
Other notable achievements include over 230 residents trained in CPR and lifesaving skills through St John Ambulance, and one of the highest concentrations of defibrillators and bleed control kits anywhere in London. 
Old Malden also became home to the UK's first Department for Transport approved hedgehog crossing signs, a scheme subsequently adopted nationally.

Road maintenance improvements in the ward were highlighted by the AA as an example of best practice, and Mike has maintained a consistent record of protecting local green spaces, biodiversity and conservation areas, alongside a zero tolerance approach to fly-tipping and environmental damage.

His working style is straightforward: listen, act, deliver, and remain accountable to residents throughout. He is known for quiet delivery over political noise, with a preference for tangible improvements over headline-chasing.

At t

I have lived in the Borough since 2014. I currently work for an insurance company in Human Resources and I am Chair of Governors at Hollyfield Secondary School in Surbiton. I have two children who both go to school in the Borough. I am passionate about supporting young people, and if elected I will work to reopen the youth clubs that the Lib Dems have closed, as well as working hard for all the residents of Old Malden.

I have lived in the Borough since 2011. I have worked for over 40 years in various roles in the social housing sector, with a focus most recently on tackling homelessness and rough sleeping. I am passionate about housing issues and, if elected, would work hard to increase the proportion of affordable housing included in new developments in the borough, and to ensure that the Council takes full advantage of government initiatives to reduce homelessness. I have experience of being a Labour Councillor in LB Richmond from 1998-2002. I am a season ticket holder at Fulham FC and a member of Surrey CCC.

I grew up in New Malden and have lived in the Borough all my life. I am a trade unionist and political researcher and know that we can do better for our area. Both the local Conservatives and Lib Dems are complacent and have failed to make the most of our outstanding local resources to promote the borough either economically or as a great place to live. Residents deserve so much more, and restoring a Labour voice to the council would bring in new thinking, challenge existing cronyism and ensure a fresh commitment to support all who live here.

I have lived in Surbiton for the last four years, and feel lucky to have found such a welcoming community in such a lovely area. I believe we can do better as a borough than the options currently provided by the Liberal Democrats and the Conservatives and as a result am excited to be standing as a Labour candidate in this election. I am very proud of the manifesto we are running on, and as your councillor would particularly fight to see an increase in truly affordable homes in the borough. I feel lucky to be living here and more people should have the same opportunity.

I have lived in Kingston for more than 30 years. I am a retired local solicitor and remain closely connected to the community I have served throughout my career.

I am particularly interested in supporting both young and older residents with access to legal advice and guidance. My children attended local primary and secondary schools, and I am proud of my long-standing ties to the area.

My priorities include improving the local environment and street cleanliness, cracking down on graffiti and fly-tipping, and taking firm action on vandalism. Kingston Labour is committed to a cleaner, safer borough, stronger neighbourhood services, and better value for residents.

Kingston needs stronger accountability and more transparent decision-making, where residents are properly listened to and services are delivered consistently. Kingston Labour will put local people first and focus on delivering real results for our community.

Andrew Wooldridge lives in Berrylands and his children attend local schools.

Andrew is working to promote business in Kingston, and is standing to help make our Borough fairer.

Today, I am proud to be raising my young family in Tolworth. I was born and raised in Chessington and have lived in every part of the borough, giving me a deep, personal understanding of our community and the issues that matter most to local residents.

I’ve been in Kingston since 1963, attending Beverley Boys (now Coombe) and despite a difficult start with my Father dying of a drug overdose followed by my elder Brother from alcohol addiction, I’ve had a rewarding life. Whilst working as a mechanic on the Portsmouth Road, I studied for a degree in the evenings at Kingston University, which enabled me to join the Civil Service, winning an ‘Outstanding Achievement’ award for the 2012 London Games. I also won the Policy Award at the 2015 Civil Service Awards and served with the World Health Organisation. Over the years I’ve always tried to give back to the community that has been so kind to me in my hardest times, volunteering with Kingston Rugby Club & Rowing Club and currently as an England Boxing coach and Secretary of Surbiton Boxing Club. It would be an honour to now serve as your councillor.

Jamila has been running her own business for the last 12 years in Kingston. In that time she has got to know the residents and issues concerning Kingston town centre. She is passionate about making Kingston a welcoming place for all by protecting green spaces, investment in parks and stopping anti-social behaviour. Jamila volunteers for a wide range of charities and community groups, working families and children to give them self confidence, including girls guiding, supporting a soup kitchen and a domestic violence charity.
